best_candidate.title: Protein Ensemble Generation Through Variational Autoencoder Latent Space Sampling

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11008089, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

journal.name: Journal of Chemical Theory and Computation

best_candidate.abstract: Mapping the ensemble of protein conformations that contribute to function and can be targeted by small molecule drugs remains an outstanding challenge. Here we explore the use of soft-introspective variational autoencoders for reducing the challenge of dimensionality in the protein structure ensemble generation problem. We convert high-dimensional protein structural data into a continuous, low-dimensional representation, carry out search in this space guided by a structure quality metric, then use RoseTTAFold to generate 3D structures. We use this approach to generate ensembles for the cancer relevant protein K-Ras, training the VAE on a subset of the available K-Ras crystal structures and MD simulation snapshots, and assessing the extent of sampling close to crystal structures withheld from training. We find that our latent space sampling procedure rapidly generates ensembles with high structural quality and is able to sample within 1 angstrom of held out crystal structures, with a consistency higher than MD simulation or AlphaFold2 prediction. The sampled structures sufficiently recapitulate the cryptic pockets in the held-out K-Ras structures to allow for small molecule docking.

data.abstract: Mapping the ensemble of protein conformations that contribute to function and can be targeted by small molecule drugs remains an outstanding challenge. Here we explore the use of soft-introspective variational autoencoders for reducing the challenge of dimensionality in the protein structure ensemble generation problem. We convert high-dimensional protein structural data into a continuous, low-dimensional representation, carry out search in this space guided by a structure quality metric, then use RoseTTAFold to generate 3D structures. We use this approach to generate ensembles for the cancer relevant protein K-Ras, training the VAE on a subset of the available K-Ras crystal structures and MD simulation snapshots, and assessing the extent of sampling close to crystal structures withheld from training. We find that our latent space sampling procedure rapidly generates ensembles with high structural quality and is able to sample within 1 angstrom of held out crystal structures, with a consistency higher than MD simulation or AlphaFold2 prediction. The sampled structures sufficiently recapitulate the cryptic pockets in the held-out K-Ras structures to allow for small molecule docking.

data.title: Data-Driven Insights into Suppressing Li₂CO₃ Formation on Doped LLZO Solid Electrolytes Using First-Principles and Machine Learning Approaches

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://api.unpaywall.org/v2/10.1149/ma2025-02291596mtgabs?email=<INSERT_YOUR_EMAIL> or https://doi.org/10.1149/ma2025-02291596mtgabs, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

data.abstract:
The interfacial formation of Li₂CO₃ on garnet-type Li₇La₃Zr₂O₁₂ (LLZO) solid-state electrolytes presents a critical barrier to achieving chemically stable and highly conductive interfaces in next-generation all-solid-state batteries [1]. This work investigates how compositional tuning, via high-entropy doping and anion-site substitution, modulates the thermodynamics and kinetics of Li₂CO₃ formation at the LLZO surface.
 We employ a multi-tiered computational approach that combines density functional theory (DFT) calculations, potential-dependent adsorption modeling, and emerging machine learning (ML) tools to analyze and predict interfacial stability. Adsorption energetics of CO₃²⁻ species are calculated across a range of doped LLZO surfaces and are coupled with Langmuir-based and Frumkin-based models to evaluate surface coverage under varying electrochemical conditions [2,3]. Additionally, a self-consistent algorithm captures the feedback between adsorbate coverage and surface potential through work function shifts.
 
 To overcome the combinatorial complexity of multi-element doping and dynamic surface reconstructions, we introduce a machine-learned framework that includes graph neural networks (GNNs) for energy prediction [4], reinforcement learning (RL) for site optimization [5], and variational autoencoders (VAEs) for structure generation [6]. Using Born-Oppenheimer molecular dynamics (BOMD) trajectories as training data, our ML models rapidly screen adsorption configurations
 
 and identify dopant chemistries that disfavor Li₂CO₃ accumulation
 
 .
 
 
 
 This integrated approach enables predictive insights into how specific dopants and charge-compensating species (e.g., F⁻ substitutions or aliovalent cations) affect the thermodynamics and kinetics of Li₂CO₃ formation.
 
 The workflow provides high-throughput guidance for designing LLZO surfaces that resist parasitic carbonate formation, directly informing experimental synthesis and interfacial treatment strategies. Overall, our findings are expected to accelerate the development of impurity-resistant garnet interfaces, directly informing experimental strategies for solid-state battery manufacturing.
 
 
 References
 
 
 1. Huo, Hanyu, et al. "Li
 2
 CO
 3
 : a critical issue for developing solid garnet batteries."
 ACS Energy Letters
 5.1 (2019): 252-262.
 
 
 2. Schmickler, Wolfgang, and Elizabeth Santos.
 Interfacial electrochemistry
 . Springer Science & Business Media, 2010.
 
 
 3. Huggins, Robert.
 Advanced batteries: materials science aspects
 . Springer Science & Business Media, 2008.
 
 
 4. Pablo-García, Sergio, et al. "Fast evaluation of the adsorption energy of organic molecules on metals via graph neural networks."
 Nature Computational Science
 3.5 (2023): 433-442.
 
 
 5. Meldgaard, Søren A., et al. "Structure prediction of surface reconstructions by deep reinforcement learning."
 Journal of Physics: Condensed Matter
 32.40 (2020): 404005.
 
 
 6.Mansoor, Sanaa, et al. "Protein ensemble generation through variational autoencoder latent space sampling."
 Journal of Chemical Theory and Computation
 20.7 (2024): 2689-2695.
